The machine does the
heavy lifting

Drop your CSV datasets. DataWeave V13 automatically detects connections, validates statistics, and writes the story — you just read it.

◎Hero EntityAuto-detects your primary identifier across up to 200 MB of data
◈InsightsStats with confidence levels & validation
∿Auto-ChartsChart.js visualisations, zero config
◉Dossier360° entity profile across all datasets
📋ReportExecutive summary + printable poster
◈
How this works: DataWeave joined your datasets via the detected Hero Entity key, then cross-referenced categorical columns (e.g. species, SES rank) against quantitative columns (e.g. marks, logins). Each insight below required a minimum sample of 30 records per group and a minimum variance threshold before being surfaced. Confidence levels indicate statistical robustness.
📖 Data Fiction

Story Card

Writing your Data Fiction Card…
⬡
Schema Map: Nodes are datasets. Edges show auto-detected joins — purple = Entity Match (student_id/module_code), amber = Temporal (academic_year), blue = Schema Join (programme_code). Edge thickness = match volume. Click any node to see all graphs it enables. Click an edge to inspect the join in detail.
⬡

Click any node or edge on the map to see connection details, shared columns, coverage health, and a plain-English explanation of the relationship.

All Connection Coverage

∿
Auto-Graphing Engine: DataWeave scanned your datasets for linkable numeric and categorical columns. Charts are only generated when there are at least 5 distinct groups or 20+ data points. Each chart type was chosen based on the data shape — bar charts for group comparisons, scatter for correlations.
◉
Dossier Generator: Enter any Hero Entity ID or name. DataWeave traces that identifier across all loaded datasets and assembles every record into a single unified profile card — no manual querying needed.
🏛️
Department Explorer: Select a department to see its full demographic breakdown, academic performance, and module catalogue. Data is joined live from dim_programmes, dim_students, fact_assessment, and dim_modules.
⊞
Heatmap formatting: Cells are automatically coloured — green for high marks (70+), amber for mid (50–69), red for low (<50). Status columns become colour-coded pills. Key columns (shared identifiers) are highlighted in purple.
Mark ≥ 70 Mark 50–69 Mark < 50 Key column
📋
Executive Report: A structured summary of the entire dataset ecosystem — total records, hero entity, connection health, and the top 3 validated insights. Use Generate Poster to export a printable one-page data story card.
🔬
Student Grouping: Groups students into behavioural archetypes based on how they engage and perform — using whichever of these signals are present in your data: assessment marks, online (VLE) activity, attendance, sense of belonging, and socio-economic background. Click a cluster card to browse its students.

VLE Logins vs Assessment Mark — by Cluster

Students in Cluster

⚖️
Exploratory Comparison Mode: Define two distinct student groups using the filters below (e.g. Group A = Dwarf students, Group B = Elf students; or Group A = First-gen, Group B = Non-first-gen). Then click Compare Groups. DataWeave will compute 13 outcome metrics for each group, highlight statistically notable differences, and generate an AI-written summary. Requires dim_students, fact_assessment, and at least one fact table. Tip: groups should differ by at least one filter — if both are set to "All", the comparison will be identical.
Group Builder — set different filters on each side to compare

📊 AI Analysis
📊
Generating analysis…
Visualisations
Degree Classification Distribution
Normalised Metric Radar
🔮
Scenario Explorer: Model the projected impact of targeted interventions before committing institutional resources. Each scenario uses observed Stonegrove data to quantify the expected change in student outcomes. Upload your CSVs, then run any scenario to generate a data-backed policy projection with AI summary.
🔮
Upload data to run scenarios
Scenario Explorer requires fact_enrolment_survey, fact_progression, fact_assessment, and dim_students to be loaded.
🎯
Belonging Intervention
What if we intervened with every student whose belonging score dropped below a threshold?
Scenario A
Students with low peer-belonging scores are at elevated risk of disengagement and withdrawal. This scenario identifies students who reported a belonging_peers score below your threshold, compares their outcomes against the full population, and projects how many would be retained if a targeted intervention reduced their fail/withdrawal rate by a conservative 25%.
Read it as: a what-if, not a forecast. The 25% reduction is an assumption — run it to see the cautious-to-optimistic range. It also assumes low belonging causes withdrawal (it may be a symptom), and that the students flagged are the ones an intervention would reach.
3.0
📊Generating policy summary…
🔧
Killer Module Reform
What if a high-fail module saw improved average marks?
Scenario B
Some modules disproportionately drive year-level failures. Select a module and simulate a mark improvement — perhaps through redesigned assessments, added tutorials, or better feedback. The engine uses the observed correlation between module marks and year outcomes to project how many additional students would pass the year.
Read it as: directional, not causal. It assumes the historical link between module marks and passing the year would hold if you raised marks — but the correlation may reflect students who were already stronger, not the module itself. Treat the number as "worth investigating," not a guaranteed yield.
+10 pp
📊Generating policy summary…
⚖️
SES Levelling
What if low-SES students achieved the same marks as a higher-SES group?
Scenario C
Socio-economic disadvantage often manifests as a persistent mark gap. This scenario closes that gap — modelling what the First Class graduation rate would look like if students from SES ranks 1–3 achieved marks comparable to a target SES group. The projection isolates the portion of the attainment gap attributable to the mark differential.
Read it as: a ceiling, not a plan. It models only the mark-attributable slice of the gap and assumes marks could be fully equalised — which no single intervention does. The remaining gap (access, retention, other drivers) is out of scope here, so the real-world effect would be smaller.
SES 6
📊Generating policy summary…

Your Academic Journey
A full picture of your time at Stonegrove — your way.
⌕
Support & Resources
You don't have to navigate university alone. These resources are here for you.
🆘 Need help right now?
If you're in crisis or worried about your safety, you don't have to wait for an appointment. These lines are open right now.
🚨
Emergency — 999
If you or someone else is in immediate danger or it's a life-threatening emergency.
Call 999
🏥
NHS 111 — Mental Health
Call 111 and select the mental health option for urgent support from a trained professional, 24/7.
Call 111, then option 2
📞
Samaritans
Whatever you're going through, someone will listen. Free from any phone, day or night — you don't have to be in crisis to call.
116 123 (free, 24/7)
✉️
Shout Text Line
Prefer not to talk? Text the word SHOUT to 85258 for free, confidential 24/7 support by text message.
Text SHOUT to 85258
💬 Talk to Someone
🧠
Student Minds
UK's student mental health charity. Resources, peer support, and research-backed tools.
studentminds.org.uk ↗
📞
Samaritans
Free to call, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. You don't have to be in crisis to reach out.
116 123 (free, 24/7)
💬
University Counselling
Confidential one-to-one sessions with a trained counsellor. Short-term and ongoing support available.
Book via student portal
🎓
Your Personal Tutor
Your academic point of contact for pastoral support, guidance, and referrals to specialist services.
Contact via student portal
🤍
Wellbeing Adviser
Drop-in and booked appointments with the university wellbeing team for non-clinical emotional and practical support.
Book a wellbeing appointment
🌙
Nightline
A confidential overnight listening service run by trained student volunteers. Check whether your university runs one.
nightline.ac.uk ↗
🩺 Health & Wellbeing
🏥
Register with a GP
Register with a local doctor or the campus health centre so you can be seen quickly when you're unwell.
Find your campus GP
♿
Disability & Accessibility
Reasonable adjustments, exam access arrangements, and advice on Disabled Students' Allowance (DSA).
Contact the disability team
😴
Wellbeing & Self-Care
Workshops and resources on sleep, stress, managing workload, and building healthy study habits.
Browse wellbeing resources
📚 Academic Support
📚
Study Skills Support
Academic writing workshops, revision techniques, note-taking strategies, and exam preparation support.
Book a workshop
📅
Extenuating Circumstances
If illness or a personal crisis affects your studies, you can request mitigation on deadlines and assessments.
Submit a request
🗣️
English Language Support
Free academic English classes and one-to-one tutorials, especially for international students.
Join a session
⚖️
Academic Appeals
Independent guidance if you want to appeal a result or academic decision. The SU can support you through it.
Get advice first
💷 Money & Practical
💰
Financial Advice
Bursaries, hardship funds, budgeting support, and advice on student finance entitlements.
Speak to the finance team
🏠
Accommodation Support
Help with housing problems, contracts, deposits, and disputes with landlords or halls.
Contact accommodation services
🌍
International & Visa
Advice on visas, immigration, working in the UK, and settling in as an international student.
Contact international advice
💼
Careers Service
CV checks, interview practice, placements, and one-to-one careers guidance — during and after your course.
Book a careers appointment
🤝 Community & Belonging
🤝
Peer Mentoring
Connect with students a year ahead of you who've been through similar challenges and know the ropes.
Join the programme
🎭
Societies & Clubs
Hundreds of student groups — sport, culture, hobbies, faith and more. One of the easiest ways to belong.
Explore the SU
🕊️
Chaplaincy & Faith
A welcoming space for students of all faiths and none — quiet rooms, community, and a listening ear.
Visit the chaplaincy
🛡️ Safety & Reporting
📣
Report + Support
Report harassment, bullying, discrimination, or sexual misconduct — named or anonymously — and access support.
Make a report
🛡️
Campus Security
On-campus security operates around the clock. Save the number in your phone for getting home safely.
24/7 campus security
🧭
Students' Union Advice
Free, independent and confidential advice — separate from the university — on academic, housing and money issues.
Contact SU advice
🔐 Your Data Rights

Stonegrove University holds data about your academic journey. You have the right to: access the data held about you (Subject Access Request), request corrections to inaccurate data, understand how your data is used for institutional decision-making, and opt out of certain types of profiling. Contact the Data Protection Officer via the student portal to exercise any of these rights.

This dashboard uses data about you across multiple datasets.
💬 Ask DataWeave Natural language queries over Stonegrove data
Try asking…

⚖️ Ethics Audit

Automated review of data ethics considerations raised by the datasets and analyses currently loaded. Flags are generated from the active data — upload more datasets to see additional considerations.

🔴 0 Serious
🟠 0 Caution
🔵 0 Information
Upload at least one dataset to run the ethics audit.

What This Dashboard Cannot See

  • A student's home circumstances, caring responsibilities, or external employment pressures
  • Mental health fluctuations that are not captured in attendance or VLE data
  • Cultural context and identity dimensions beyond the recorded categories
  • The difference between disengagement by choice and disengagement by crisis
  • Institutional racism, ableism, or other structural barriers embedded in assessment and progression systems
  • The relationships, conversations, and moments of support that shaped each student's journey

Every number in this dashboard represents a person whose full story is not in this dataset.

🗒️ Governance Log

The session log resets when the page is closed and is not transmitted anywhere. Red-list (Special Category) override justifications are kept locally in this browser as an audit trail. The dossier exports are a ready-to-read HTML document (open or print to PDF) carrying the flags, the Art.35 impact assessment, this log and a department comparison; the confidential copy adds the named at-risk register as an annex at the back. The raw-log JSON remains for machine-readable archiving.

No files processed this session.

🗺 Code Atlas — how this tool works

      every claim anchored to a source line · click any anchor to open the running file at that line
      
    


    

      
20,730lines, one file

      
21classes

      
277methods

      
1external dependency (Chart.js CDN)

    


    
This page is generated into the artifact at build time: every line number and stamp figure above and below is resolved by script against the assembled file — never typed by hand. The build was directed AI collaboration, with the owner as product, QA and architecture lead. Anchors point into the inline script of the file you are running right now; the viewer reads that script's own text, so what you see is what executes.


    

      
1 · The artifact and its source

      
DataWeave is one HTML file: stylesheet, shell, and a single inline script, with Chart.js as the only external dependency. Double-click and it runs — no install, no build, no server. That is a deliberate property, not a shortcut: the tool argues that governance machinery should be inspectable by the people it governs, and a single file is the most inspectable deployment there is.

      
Since the modularization pass the single file is a build output: an editable source tree (three shell files plus 27 JS modules — ordered concatenation units sharing one top-level scope, not ES modules) reassembles into this artifact byte-for-byte, and the full test suite (197 checks across four batteries) runs against the built file. What ships and what is edited are the same bytes, provably.

    


    

      
2 · Boot and the spine

      
Boot waits for DOMContentLoaded, guards against a failed Chart.js CDN load, constructs one instance of every engine and wires the tabs. The data path is: ingest → profile → route → analyse → publish.

      
Ingest. CSV parsing exists in three copies that must stay in lockstep — a synchronous parser, a chunked main-thread parser, and an inline Web Worker copy for large files. All three sniff the delimiter (comma / tab / semicolon, counted outside quotes), strip a UTF-8 BOM explicitly, rejoin quoted embedded newlines per RFC 4180, and accumulate a parse-metadata record (blanks skipped, ragged rows padded or truncated, replacement characters) that feeds the Data Health card.

      
Profile. Every dataset gets a per-column profile: kind (id / numeric / ordinal / boolean / date / categorical / text / constant) and semantic roles — the entity key, the measures, the dimensions, the dates — inferred from values, not names. An unnamed unique-integer column must also look near-consecutive before it is classed as an identifier, so a clean integer measure is never silently deleted. Date conventions are resolved once per column (day-first evidence wins; ambiguous slash dates default to the UK reading, and that choice is recorded, not hidden).

      
Route. The Stonegrove educational schema is detected by signature and runs the original pipeline unchanged — regression-smoked on every change since the split. Any other schema runs the generic pipeline: same statistics, same disclosure floors, profile-driven stories, charts, clustering and ethics flags. Surfaces that need machinery the schema cannot support (the risk roster, the fairness audit, the dossier) hide behind an honest note instead of rendering theatre.

      

        DataEngine → L5996
        SchemaProfiler → L5862
        delimiter sniff → L6019
        parseDateFlex → L5874
        detectHeroEntity → L6464
        data health → L6224
      

    


    

      
3 · The governance stack

      
The gate. Before a file is accepted, its column names are checked against 4 red patterns (GDPR Art. 9 special-category territory: a hard stop that can only be overridden by typing a lawful-basis justification, which is recorded) and 6 amber patterns (logged, upload proceeds). The shipped rules are constants; the effective tiers are governance settings — a rule can be retiered or disabled through the Governance Configuration panel, and every such change is audited with before/after values.

      
The floors. Two separate layers that must never be confused. The Disclosure layer is privacy: means suppress at n≤7, percentages below 22.5, displayed counts round to the nearest 5 — and it never relaxes, not even for protected groups; suppressed cells render as ·· rather than dropping rows. The confidence layer is statistics: findings below a minimum group size are flagged low-confidence — and it may relax slightly for protected groups, because silence about a minority is itself a harm.

      
Rights and scope. Students can dispute any fired signal, leave voice notes, and opt out. Opt-out has a governance-controlled scope: under the shipped default (visibility) the roster row is hidden but the score still computes — a documented limitation the impact assessment states verbatim; under processing the student is not scored at all, and the roster, back-test and fairness audit all exclude them through the same guard. The rights record travels as a checksummed export/import file with stated merge rules; the checksum is tamper-evident, not cryptographic, and says so in the record itself.

      
Config and review. The nine risk weights and ten rule tiers are editable in-app; every edit lands in the persisted audit trail with before/after values, engine caches key on the configuration signature, and the impact assessment plus every export document carry a "modified from defaults" stamp while the configuration differs from the shipped values. An external-review registry (who reviewed the red list, weights and blind-spot list; what they contested; what changed and why) renders in the Ethics tab and ships inside the governance dossier — and states plainly when it is empty: the lists are a single author's judgement until real review lands.

      
The trail and the vault. Every governance event — overrides, rights changes, scope changes, config edits, review edits, crypto events — appends to one persisted audit trail through a single entry point. The trail can optionally be encrypted at rest; while the vault is locked, new records land in a plaintext pending buffer that is folded in and wiped at the next unlock — the honest cost of an append-only trail whose key the file must not store, stated on the panel.

      
Exports and crypto. Confidential documents (the named register, the welfare report, the confidential dossier) leave only encrypted: AES-256-GCM, keys from passphrases via PBKDF2-SHA-256 at 310,000 iterations, fresh salt and IV per encryption, delivered as a self-contained decryptor page. There is no plaintext escape hatch — a missing WebCrypto context blocks the download with an honest message. Shareable and aggregate exports stay plaintext by design: the disclosure floors already did that protecting. The tool has no login screen, deliberately — a fake lock is worse than an honest open door — and its Deployment Profile panel states what a single file cannot enforce (authentication, access logging, distribution control) next to the server contract a real deployment adds.

      

        ConsentGate → L12644
        rules → L12659
        effectiveRules → L12747
        Disclosure → L4840
        studentDataRights → L5675
        opt-out scope → L5692
        rights export → L5749
        governanceConfig → L5328
        reviewLog → L5446
        DWCrypto → L4956
        AuditVault → L5152
        encrypted export → L5276
        deployment profile → L18675
      

    


    

      
4 · The statistics

      
Every two-group finding runs Welch's t-test with an exact p-value computed through the regularised incomplete beta function — the same machinery a statistics library uses, validated against scipy before integration — shown as p=0.014, never a band, with a 95% confidence interval on the gap and Cohen's d (pooled SD, by stated decision). Reported correlations carry Fisher-z intervals. Because the story feed runs dozens of tests per batch, every batch is Benjamini–Hochberg corrected: a card that is significant raw but not after correction says so on its face, and reliability counts use the corrected status. Tiny groups are refused rather than approximated; sample variance (n−1) is used everywhere in inference.

      
The k-means clustering reports a silhouette score sampled at 800 points — a stated speed trade. A part–whole guard catches the near-tautology a real dataset exposed (a component correlating with its own total): the finding is annotated and demoted, not suppressed — the reader decides, with the arithmetic named.

      

        _significance → L7364
        BH-FDR → L7344
        Welch (compare tab) → L16163
        silhouette → L15651
        measure hints → L5977
      

    


    

      
5 · The risk score, audited

      
The score is deliberately simple: hand-set integer weights on legible signals (fail-zone mark, low attendance, VLE disengagement, low belonging, first-gen + low SES, historical year failure), summed and tiered. No fitting, no learning — every point traces to a line, and since the config workstream every weight is an audited setting rather than a frozen constant. A weight of zero disables its signal entirely; setting the demographic weight to zero is the reversible, on-the-record form of removing it.

      
Two validations run against the score. The back-test scores every student with outcome data excluded (a leakage guard — using outcomes to predict outcomes would be circular) and checks that high tiers genuinely fail more. The fairness audit then asks the harder question on the same leakage-free scores: flag-rate parity, false-positive-rate parity and calibration per protected group, behind the disclosure floors, plus a counterfactual that rescores the identical cohort without the demographic weight and shows exactly who moves tier. The impact assessment carries the audit as a partial mitigation by design — measuring disparity is not removing it. The staff roster shows tier and signals; the 0–100 number itself is withheld from every surface, staff and student alike, because a number invites ranking.

      

        _computeRiskScore → L6709
        shipped weights → L5315
        back-test → L6929
        fairness audit → L7010
        fairness panel → L19172
      

    


    

      
6 · The student side

      
Student mode re-renders the tool from the measured person's seat: a dashboard in neutral language, a journey timeline, a support directory, and the My Data Rights panel — every fired signal listed with a dispute channel and a voice channel, the opt-out toggle with scope-aware copy, and a "take your record with you" export. The impact assessment is readable from here too: the measured can read the measurement's own risk assessment of itself.

      

        StudentModeManager → L17404
        journey → L14504
        role views → L13592
      

    


    

      
7 · How it is tested

      
A jsdom harness drives the real classes headlessly: 197 checks across four batteries (governance config & review, crypto/trust, rights & state, generic smoke), plus a 38-check hostile-input torture suite and one genuinely external dataset driven end-to-end during the messy-data workstream. Statistical code was validated against scipy gold values before integration. Every change since the dual-mode split has shipped with a Stonegrove regression smoke proving the original pipeline byte-identical at defaults.

    


    

      
8 · The ledger — what remains honestly raw

      
    
        
  • The file cannot authenticate anyone. Whoever opens it holds the session; encryption protects artifacts at rest, nothing more. Authentication, access logging and distribution control are a server's job — the RemoteAdapter REST contract documents the integration point, and the adapter is a stub until that server exists.
    • 
        
  • The external-review registry ships empty. Until real reviewers' positions land in it, the red list, the weights and the blind-spot list are one author's judgement — every relevant surface says so.
    • 
        
  • The rights-record checksum is tamper-evident, not cryptographic; signing keys cannot live in a file anyone can read.
    • 
        
  • While the audit vault is locked, new records sit in a plaintext pending buffer until the next unlock — stated on the panel and in the log export.
    • 
        
  • Ambiguous slash dates default to a UK day-first reading the file does not declare; genuinely month-first data with no day above 12 is misread. Irreducible without a locale hint; recorded, not hidden.
    • 
        
  • Fairness metrics are single observed proportions with no interval on the parity gaps; the silhouette is sampled; displayed counts are rounded to the nearest 5 even for small honest datasets — the privacy layer working as specified.
    • 
        
  • Generic mode gets the statistics but not the conscience: no tiered score exists on an arbitrary schema, so the fairness and rights machinery honestly declines rather than generalising.
    • 
      

    


    
Verify against the file, never the docs — the anchors above open the file you are running. The full two-voice Code Atlas (design decisions, their reasoning, and the cost each accepts) ships separately as the thesis appendix.
